name: Tordesillas y Rueda desde Valdestillas – circular por Simancas y río Adaja
region_intro: Road cycling around Valdestillas offers diverse landscapes across the Valladolid province, characterized by flat to gently undulating plains. The terrain features modest climbs, making it accessible for various skill levels. Routes often traverse rural environments, including riverine landscapes along the Cega and Adaja, pine forests, and extensive agricultural plains. Many paths connect historic towns, enhancing the cycling experience. Best road cycling routes around Valdestillas The most popular road cycling route is Tordesillas y Rueda desde Valdestillas – circular por Simancas y río Adaja, a 50.0 miles (80.5 km) trail that takes 4 hours 38 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the river Adaja and passes through historic areas. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Simancas Stone Bridge – Castillo de Simancas loop from Viana, a moderate 43.7 miles (70.4 km) path. This route features flat to gently undulating plains and connects to historic towns. Local road cyclists also love the Simancas – Castillo de Simancas loop from Valdestillas, a 53.0 miles (85.2 km) trail leading through agricultural plains and past historic towns, often completed in about 3 hours 44 minutes. Road cycling around Valdestillas is defined by riverine landscapes, pine forests, and agricultural plains. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy to challenging excursions. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Valdestillas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Valdestillas?
A: There are nearly 20 road cycling routes around Valdestillas, offering diverse options for cyclists. These include 2 easy routes, 14 moderate routes, and 1 difficult route, catering to various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Valdestillas?
A: The terrain around Valdestillas is generally characterized by flat to gently undulating plains, making it accessible for a wide range of cyclists. You'll encounter modest climbs, riverine landscapes along the Cega and Adaja, pine forests, and extensive agricultural plains, providing varied scenery for your rides.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, Valdestillas offers routes suitable for families and beginners. The generally flat to gently undulating terrain is ideal for leisurely rides. For an easy option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24598143" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Simancas Stone Bridge – Simancas loop from Colagón&lt;/a&gt;, which is 20.5 miles (33 km) long and features minimal elevation gain.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Valdestillas?
A: Many of the road cycling routes around Valdestillas are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end your ride in the same location. An excellent example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1152184942/tordesillas-y-rueda-desde-valdestillas-circular-por-simancas-y-rio-adaja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tordesillas y Rueda desde Valdestillas – circular por Simancas y río Adaja&lt;/a&gt;, a 50.0-mile (80.5 km) moderate route.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural features can I see along the routes?
A: Road cycling routes often incorporate views of historical attractions and natural features. You can expect to see landmarks such as the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5837382" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Simancas&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2814203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Simancas Stone Bridge&lt;/a&gt;. Many paths also follow riverine landscapes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2689667" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cega River Trails&lt;/a&gt;, and pass through pine forests and agricultural plains.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Valdestillas?
A: The region is generally pleasant for road cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer particularly scenic rides, especially along river banks where autumn foliage can be vibrant. The relatively flat terrain makes it enjoyable even in cooler months, though summer can be warm.

Q: Are there longer distance road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, Valdestillas offers several longer distance routes for those looking for extended rides. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31535572" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Simancas – Castillo de Simancas loop from Valdestillas&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 53.0-mile (85.2 km) route, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31469650" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tudela del Duero – Tudela de Duero Bridge loop from Viana&lt;/a&gt; covers 47.3 miles (76.1 km).

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Valdestillas?
A: Valdestillas and nearby towns often have public parking areas suitable for cyclists. Given the rural nature of many routes, you can typically find convenient parking in the starting villages or designated spots, especially when accessing routes that connect historic towns.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Valdestillas?
A: Road cyclists frequently praise the diverse landscapes, which include riverine environments, pine forests, and agricultural plains. The network of routes is appreciated for offering options for different ability levels, from easy to more challenging excursions, and for connecting historic towns via roads with little traffic.

Q: Are there any cafes or amenities along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes connect or pass through historic towns like Tordesillas, Rueda, and Simancas. These towns typically offer amenities such as cafes, restaurants, and shops where you can stop for refreshments or supplies during your ride.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: While the region is known for its generally flat to gently undulating terrain, there is at least one difficult route available for experienced cyclists seeking a greater challenge. These routes may feature more significant elevation gains or longer distances to test endurance.

Q: Can I reach Valdestillas by public transport with my bike?
A: Public transport options to Valdestillas, particularly those accommodating bicycles, may be limited. It's advisable to check local bus or train services in the Valladolid province for their specific policies regarding bicycle transport before planning your trip.
tips:
The Church of Our Lady of the Assumption in Rueda (Valladolid) is a prominent 18th-century Baroque church that, together with its Renaissance tower, shines in the heart of the town.

It was designed by the architect Manuel Serrano and built in the mid-18th century; the main portal and altarpiece are attributed to Pedro de Sierra.

The five-tiered tower dates from the earlier Renaissance construction and was preserved in the new structure.

Declared a Site of Cultural Interest (monument) on October 17, 2013.
Entering the town of Tordesillas, the medieval bridge over the Duero River offers incredible views of the city's landscape.

It is the place that throughout history has welcomed the community in its public activities: market, medieval games, bullfighting celebrations, etc.
Square of medieval origin. Its shape dates back to the time of the Catholic Monarchs, when they ordered the construction of a larger square in Tordesillas in accordance with the importance of the town. For this reason, a series of buildings will be demolished until the square acquires this almost square shape surrounded by arcades.
Quiet country road towards Geria surrounded by cultivated fields. The landscape keeps changing with the seasons.
This impressive castle was built in the 15th century by the Admiral of Castilla Don Fadrique Enríquez, later it passed into the hands of the monarchy.
Pedestrian bridge over the Pisuerga river. On the town side there is a bar and a restaurant with a good atmosphere.

Crossing the Adaja river near the Valdestillas train station, the old Roman bridge is eclipsed by the modern train viaduct. There is a very nice river beach for swimming nearby if you want to rest and cool off.

waypoints.name: Parish of the Assumption of Our Lady of Rueda
Roman Bridge over the Adaja River
